title: Fidelity

infobox.value: * [Endowment](/wiki/Endowment_(philosophy) "Endowment (philosophy)") * [Moral character](/wiki/Moral_character "Moral character") * *[Nicomachean Ethics](/wiki/Nicomachean_Ethics "Nicomachean Ethics")* * [Positive psychology](/wiki/Positive_psychology "Positive psychology") * [Trait theory](/wiki/Trait_theory "Trait theory") * [Virtue ethics](/wiki/Virtue_ethics "Virtue ethics")

is the quality of [faithfulness](/wiki/Faithfulness "Faithfulness") or [loyalty](/wiki/Loyalty "Loyalty"). Its original meaning regarded [duty](/wiki/Duty "Duty") in a broader sense than the related concept of *[fealty](/wiki/Fealty "Fealty")*. Both derive from the [Latin](/wiki/Latin "Latin") word *fidēlis*, meaning "faithful or loyal". In the [City of London](/wiki/City_of_London "City of London") financial markets it has traditionally been used in the sense encompassed in the motto "my word is my bond".

From Wikipedia, the free encyclopedia

Quality of faithfulness or loyalty to another person or group

For other uses, see [Fidelity (disambiguation)](/wiki/Fidelity_(disambiguation) "Fidelity (disambiguation)").

|  |  |
| --- | --- |
|  | This article **may lack focus or be about [more than one topic](/wiki/Wikipedia:REORGANIZE "Wikipedia:REORGANIZE")**. Please help [improve this article](https://en.wikipedia.org/w/index.php?title=Fidelity&action=edit), possibly by [splitting](/wiki/Wikipedia:Splitting "Wikipedia:Splitting") it or creating a [disambiguation page](/wiki/Wikipedia:Disambiguation "Wikipedia:Disambiguation"). Relevant discussion may be found on the [talk page](/wiki/Talk:Fidelity "Talk:Fidelity"). *(March 2023)* *([Learn how and when to remove this message](/wiki/Help:Maintenance_template_removal "Help:Maintenance template removal"))* |

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/6/6d/4243_-_Venezia_-_Palazzo_ducale_-_Capitello_28_-_In_fidelitate_nullli_gero_-_Foto_Giovanni_Dall%27Orto%2C_30-Jul-2008.jpg/250px-4243_-_Venezia_-_Palazzo_ducale_-_Capitello_28_-_In_fidelitate_nullli_gero_-_Foto_Giovanni_Dall%27Orto%2C_30-Jul-2008.jpg)](/wiki/File:4243_-_Venezia_-_Palazzo_ducale_-_Capitello_28_-_In_fidelitate_nullli_gero_-_Foto_Giovanni_Dall%27Orto,_30-Jul-2008.jpg)

[Palazzo Ducale in Venice](/wiki/Doge%27s_Palace,_Venice "Doge's Palace, Venice"): [capital # 28](https://commons.wikimedia.org/wiki/Category:Capitals_in_the_Doge%27s_Palace_(Venice)_-_28 "commons:Category:Capitals in the Doge's Palace (Venice) - 28") in the porch, featuring Virtues and vices — **In fidelitate nulli gero** (Fidelity)

**Fidelity** is the quality of [faithfulness](/wiki/Faithfulness "Faithfulness") or [loyalty](/wiki/Loyalty "Loyalty"). Its original meaning regarded [duty](/wiki/Duty "Duty") in a broader sense than the related concept of *[fealty](/wiki/Fealty "Fealty")*. Both derive from the [Latin](/wiki/Latin "Latin") word *fidēlis*, meaning "faithful or loyal". In the [City of London](/wiki/City_of_London "City of London") financial markets it has traditionally been used in the sense encompassed in the motto "my word is my bond".

## In moral philosophy

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Fidelity&action=edit&section=1 "Edit section: In moral philosophy")]

In moral philosophy, fidelity refers to a person who keeps agreements. Strong fidelity refers to a person who keeps agreements even if not mutually beneficial to the other person.[[1]](#cite_note-1)

## Audio and electronics

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Fidelity&action=edit&section=2 "Edit section: Audio and electronics")]

See also: [Sound recording and reproduction](/wiki/Sound_recording_and_reproduction "Sound recording and reproduction")

In audio, "fidelity" denotes how accurately a copy reproduces its source. In the 1950s, the terms "[high fidelity](/wiki/High_fidelity "High fidelity")" or "hi-fi" were popularized for equipment and recordings which exhibited more accurate [sound reproduction](/wiki/Sound_reproduction "Sound reproduction").[[2]](#cite_note-2) For example, a worn [gramophone record](/wiki/Gramophone_record "Gramophone record") will have a lower fidelity than one in good condition, and a recording made by a low budget record company in the early 20th century is likely to have significantly less audio fidelity than a good modern recording.[[3]](#cite_note-3) Similarly in [electronics](/wiki/Electronics "Electronics"), fidelity refers to the correspondence of the output signal to the input signal, rather than sound quality, as in the popular internet connection technology "[Wi-Fi](/wiki/Wi-Fi "Wi-Fi")".[[4]](#cite_note-4)

The term "[lo-fi](/wiki/Lo-fi_music "Lo-fi music")" has existed since at least the 1950s, shortly after the acceptance of "hi-fi", but its definition evolved continuously between the 1970s and 2000s. In the 1976 edition of the *Oxford Dictionary*,[*[clarification needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Please_clarify "Wikipedia:Please clarify")*] lo-fi was added under the definition of "sound production less good in quality than '[hi-fi](/wiki/Hi-fi "Hi-fi")'", and in the glossary of the 1977 book *The Tuning of the World*, was defined as "unfavourable signal-to-noise ratio". In 2003, the *Oxford Dictionary* added a second definition: "A genre of rock music characterized by minimal production, giving a raw and unsophisticated sound." A third was added in 2008: "Unpolished, amateurish, or technologically unsophisticated, esp. as a deliberate aesthetic choice."[[5]](#cite_note-5)[[6]](#cite_note-6)

## Scientific modelling and simulation

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Fidelity&action=edit&section=3 "Edit section: Scientific modelling and simulation")]

In the fields of [scientific modelling](/wiki/Scientific_modelling "Scientific modelling") and [simulation](/wiki/Simulation "Simulation"), fidelity refers to the degree to which a model or simulation reproduces the state and behaviour of a real world object, feature or condition. Fidelity is therefore a measure of the realism of a model or simulation.[[7]](#cite_note-SISO-7) Simulation fidelity has also been described in the past as "degree of similarity".[[8]](#cite_note-HaysSinger-8) In quantum mechanics and optics,[[9]](#cite_note-9) the fidelity of a field is calculated as an [overlap integral](/wiki/Orbital_overlap "Orbital overlap") of the field of interest with a reference or target field.

## Program evaluation

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Fidelity&action=edit&section=4 "Edit section: Program evaluation")]

In the field of [program evaluation](/wiki/Program_evaluation "Program evaluation"), the term fidelity denotes how closely a set of procedures were implemented as they were supposed to have been. For example, it is difficult to draw conclusions from a study about formative assessment in school classrooms if the teachers are not able or willing to follow the procedures they received in training.[[10]](#cite_note-ODonnell-10)

## Translation

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Fidelity&action=edit&section=5 "Edit section: Translation")]

Main article: [Translation § Fidelity and transparency](/wiki/Translation#Fidelity_and_transparency "Translation")

In [translation](/wiki/Translation "Translation"), *fidelity* is the extent to which a translation accurately renders the [meaning](/wiki/Meaning_(linguistics) "Meaning (linguistics)") of the source text, without distortion. It is contrasted with *transparency*, which is the extent to which a translation appears to a native speaker of the target language to have originally been written in that language.

## See also

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Fidelity&action=edit&section=6 "Edit section: See also")]

[![logo](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/f/fa/Wikiquote-logo.svg/40px-Wikiquote-logo.svg.png)](/wiki/File:Wikiquote-logo.svg)

Wikiquote has quotations related to ***[Fidelity](https://en.wikiquote.org/wiki/Special:Search/Fidelity "q:Special:Search/Fidelity")***.

* [Classism](/wiki/Classism "Classism") – Discrimination on the basis of social classPages displaying short descriptions of redirect targets
* [Erikson's theory of personality](/wiki/Erik_Erikson#Erikson's_theory_of_personality "Erik Erikson") – Erikson's fidelity is the ability to commit to others and acceptance of others
* [Infidelity](/wiki/Infidelity "Infidelity") – Cheating, adultery, or having an affair
* [Knighthood](/wiki/Knighthood "Knighthood") – Honorary title awarded for service to a state or churchPages displaying short descriptions of redirect targets
* [Monasticism](/wiki/Monasticism "Monasticism") – Religious way of life
* [Rankism](/wiki/Rankism "Rankism") – Rank-based discrimination
